children.value: Images as Vectors, Patches as Points

children.label: Images as Vectors, Patches as Points

children.value: Chapter 6 introduced L2 normalisation and mean subtraction as tools for invariant matching. This chapter explains the geometry behind those operations — and reveals that Pearson correlation is just a dot product between unit-mean vectors.
8.1 An Image Patch Is a Vector

children.label: 8.1 An Image Patch Is a Vector

children.html: 3×33 \times 33×3

children.value: grayscale patch has 9 pixel values. Unroll them into a column vector:

children.html: x=[x1,x2,…,x9]⊤∈R9\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_9]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^9x=[x1​,x2​,…,x9​]⊤∈R9
3×33 \times 33×3

children.value: patch is a different point. Comparing two patches means measuring the distance or angle between two points in this space.

children.html: m×nm \times nm×n
x∈Rmn\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{mn}x∈Rmn

children.value: . High-resolution patches live in high-dimensional spaces — a

children.html: 64×6464 \times 6464×64
R4096\mathbb{R}^{4096}R4096
x⋅y=∑ixiyi=∥x∥∥y∥cos⁡θ\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = \sum_i x_i y_i = \|\mathbf{x}\| \|\mathbf{y}\| \cos\thetax⋅y=i∑​xi​yi​=∥x∥∥y∥cosθ

children.value: The dot product measures pixel-by-pixel agreement. But it depends on the magnitude of both vectors — a brighter patch has a larger dot product with everything, regardless of shape similarity.

children.html: ∥x∥=∑ixi2\|\mathbf{x}\| = \sqrt{\sum_i x_i^2}∥x∥=i∑​xi2​​

children.value: Dividing by the norm gives a

children.html: x^=x/∥x∥\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} / \|\mathbf{x}\|x^=x/∥x∥
∥x^∥=1\|\hat{\mathbf{x}}\| = 1∥x^∥=1

children.value: All unit vectors lie on the surface of the unit hypersphere. Their dot product is:

children.html: x^⋅y^=cos⁡θ\hat{\mathbf{x}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \cos\thetax^⋅y^​=cosθ
θ\thetaθ

children.value: measures the angle — independent of magnitude. A contrast change (

children.html: y=ax\mathbf{y} = a\mathbf{x}y=ax

children.value: ) does not change the angle, so it does not change cosine similarity.
This is the geometric interpretation of L2 normalisation from Chapter 6.
8.4 Mean Subtraction — Projecting Out the Brightness Direction

children.label: 8.4 Mean Subtraction — Projecting Out the Brightness Direction

children.html: 1=[1,1,…,1]⊤\mathbf{1} = [1, 1, \ldots, 1]^\top1=[1,1,…,1]⊤

children.value: points in the “uniform brightness” direction. Projecting

children.html: x\mathbf{x}x
1\mathbf{1}1

children.value: gives the mean; subtracting it removes the mean:

children.html: x~=x−xˉ1\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} - \bar{x}\mathbf{1}x~=x−xˉ1
x\mathbf{x}x
1\mathbf{1}1
y=x+b1\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{x} + b\mathbf{1}y=x+b1
1\mathbf{1}1

children.value: After mean subtraction, the dot product is unaffected by uniform brightness changes. This is the geometric interpretation of mean subtraction from Chapter 6.
8.5 Pearson Correlation = Cosine of Mean-Subtracted Vectors

children.label: 8.5 Pearson Correlation = Cosine of Mean-Subtracted Vectors

children.value: This geometric view makes the invariance obvious — and makes the ceiling obvious: once the pixel grid shifts (rotation, scale), the vectors

children.html: x~\tilde{\mathbf{x}}x~
y~\tilde{\mathbf{y}}y~​

children.value: have different components shuffled, and no amount of normalisation restores alignment.

children.html: x⋅y=0\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = 0x⋅y=0

children.value: — they are geometrically perpendicular, carrying completely independent information.

children.html: QQQ
∥Qx∥=∥x∥,(Qx)⋅(Qy)=x⋅y\|Q\mathbf{x}\| = \|\mathbf{x}\|, \quad (Q\mathbf{x}) \cdot (Q\mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}∥Qx∥=∥x∥,(Qx)⋅(Qy)=x⋅y

children.value: The Fourier transform is an orthogonal transform — it decomposes an image into orthogonal frequency components (the basis of Chapter 1’s frequency analysis) while preserving energy (Parseval’s theorem).
8.7 The Manifold Hypothesis — Why High-Dimensional Pixel Space Is Nearly Empty

children.label: 8.7 The Manifold Hypothesis — Why High-Dimensional Pixel Space Is Nearly Empty

children.html: m×nm \times nm×n
Rmn\mathbb{R}^{mn}Rmn
64×6464 \times 6464×64
R4096\mathbb{R}^{4096}R4096

children.value: . The number of possible images is
occupy a tiny, thin slice of this space. Most points in

children.html: R4096\mathbb{R}^{4096}R4096

children.value: are random noise — not images of anything real. The set of natural images forms a low-dimensional
embedded in the high-dimensional pixel space.

, and it explains why learned features work: CNNs learn to map the high-dimensional pixel space to a lower-dimensional representation that captures where you are on the natural image manifold — not where you are in the raw pixel cube.
— All 4 parts: vectors and dot product → norms and similarity → orthogonality → transforms.
